name: SAFET
title: SAFE-T — Suicide Assessment Five-Step Evaluation and Triage
publisher: SPiER Project (FHIR translation of the SAMHSA SAFE-T pocket card)
description: FHIR Questionnaire representation of the SAMHSA Suicide Assessment Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (SAFE-T). SAFE-T is a structured clinical formulation — not a scored survey — that guides a clinician through five steps: (1) identify risk factors, (2) identify protective factors, (3) conduct a suicide inquiry, (4) determine a risk level and intervention based on clinical judgment, and (5) document the risk level, rationale, treatment plan, means-safety counseling, and follow-up. Source: SAMHSA SAFE-T pocket card (PEP24-01-036).
purpose: Produce a documented, machine-actionable suicide-risk LEVEL with rationale and disposition. Because the risk level is a clinical-judgment formulation that maps onto the common tiers, the derived Observation's value binds DIRECTLY to the SPiER shared suicide-risk-tier ValueSet — SAFE-T lands on the concept layer without a per-instrument crosswalk. The risk-level item carries the generic LOINC 93374-7 ('Suicide risk level'); risk-factor and protective-factor checklists use SPiER-local CodeSystems marked 'no-standard-binding'.
copyright: The SAFE-T (Suicide Assessment Five-Step Evaluation and Triage) pocket card is a public resource of the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), developed with Screening for Mental Health, Inc. and Douglas Jacobs, MD. Distributed free by SAMHSA; no permission or fee is required for use.

code.display: Suicide Assessment Five-Step Evaluation and Triage (SAFE-T) panel

item.text: Step 1 — Identify risk factors (note those that can be modified to reduce risk; select all that apply)

valueCoding.display: History of mental health concerns, particularly depression and other mood disorders
Access to lethal means, including firearms
Knowing someone who died by suicide, particularly a family member
Lack of access to behavioral health care

item.text: Step 2 — Identify protective factors (note those that can be enhanced; select all that apply). Note: protective factors, even if present, may not counteract significant acute risk.

valueCoding.display: Connectedness to people, family, community, and social supports
Life skills (problem-solving, coping, emotional regulation, ability to adapt to change)
Self-esteem and a sense of purpose or meaning in life
Cultural, religious, or personal beliefs that discourage suicide

item.text: Step 3 — Conduct suicide inquiry (specific questioning about thoughts, plans, behaviors, intent)
Ideation present?
Ideation — frequency, intensity, duration (in last 48 hours, past month, and worst ever)
Plan present?
Plan — timing, location, lethality, availability, preparatory acts
Behaviors — past attempts, aborted attempts, rehearsals versus non-suicidal self-injurious actions
Intent present?
Intent — extent to which the patient expects to carry out the plan and believes it to be lethal. Explore ambivalence: reasons to die versus reasons to live.
Step 4 — Determine risk level & intervention (based on clinical judgment after completing steps 1–3; develop a safety plan for all individuals at low, moderate, and high risk)
Risk level

valueCoding.display: Low — thoughts of death; no plan, intent, or behavior; manageable risk factors, strong protective factors
Moderate — suicidal ideation with plan, but no intent or behavior; multiple risk factors, few protective factors
High — suicidal ideation with plan, method, and intent to carry out; severe symptoms or acute precipitating event

item.text: Intervention / triage (e.g. outpatient referral with warm handoff; admission; emergency psychiatric treatment in a secure setting; provide 988 Lifeline)
Step 5 — Document (risk level and rationale; treatment plan; counseling on access to lethal means; follow-up)
Rationale for the risk-level determination
Does your clinical judgment override the risk level suggested by the risk/protective factors and suicide inquiry?
If yes, document the reason for the clinical-judgment override
Treatment plan to address/reduce current risk (safety plan, medication, psychotherapy, contact with significant others, consultation, etc.)
Counseling on access to lethal means provided?
Follow-up plan
